text:
HEMATOLOGY / MEDICAL ONCOLOGY
OFFICE VISIT -- MULTIDISCIPLINARY CASE REVIEW AND TREATMENT ASSESSMENT

ATTENDING PHYSICIAN: [Attending Oncologist]
DEPARTMENT: Medical Oncology / Genitourinary Oncology

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Raymond Kowalski is a 64-year-old male with metastatic clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC) with sarcomatoid differentiation presenting today for multidisciplinary review of an ongoing discordant treatment response pattern on Opdivo maintenance therapy. He is approximately 6 months into first-line systemic therapy (Yervoy/nivo induction x4 cycles followed by Opdivo maintenance).

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Kowalski was initially diagnosed approximately 6 months ago with stage IV clear cell RCC, right-sided, T3bN1M1. His tumor was notable for WHO/ISUP grade 4 disease with approximately 22% sarcomatoid differentiation on final nephrectomy pathology. Molecular profiling by FoundationOne CDx demonstrated a high-risk genomic profile including biallelic VHL loss, PBRM1 and BAP1 co-loss (both pathogenic loss-of-function mutations), SETD2 mutation, CDKN2A/B homozygous deletion, MET amplification (copy number 6.8), and TP53 R248W. PD-L1 CPS was 18 by 22C3. He underwent cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy with retroperitoneal lymph node dissection approximately 2 months after diagnosis, with R0 resection of the primary, extraction of the renal vein thrombus, and removal of 8 retroperitoneal nodes (3 of 8 positive). Residual disease at the start of systemic therapy consisted of bilateral pulmonary nodules and newly emergent retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease.

He was initiated on Yervoy 1 mg/kg + nivo 3 mg/kg IV every 3 weeks per the CheckMate 214 regimen. He tolerated this well aside from grade 2 immune-related hypothyroidism (TSH 18.4 mIU/L, free T4 0.6 ng/dL) that developed after cycle 2, managed with levothyroxine 75 mcg daily without interruption of immunotherapy. He completed all 4 planned induction cycles.

First restaging CT at approximately 3 months (completion of induction) revealed a discordant or "mixed" response: partial response in the pulmonary target lesions (right lower lobe nodule from 1.4 cm to 0.9 cm, right upper lobe from 0.6 cm to 0.4 cm), but simultaneously, new retroperitoneal and pelvic nodal disease (new 1.6 cm right common iliac node, new 2.1 cm aortocaval nodal conglomerate). After multidisciplinary discussion at that time, given concern for possible pseudoprogression versus a true discordant biological response, the decision was made to continue nivo maintenance at 480 mg flat dose every 4 weeks and reimage in 6 weeks.

The second restaging CT (performed approximately 6 weeks after the first restaging, today approximately 6 months total from immunotherapy initiation) now shows the following:
- Pulmonary lesions continuing to respond: RLL nodule down to 0.7 cm, RUL 0.3 cm, LLL 0.7 cm (slight decrease)
- Retroperitoneal/pelvic nodal disease clearly progressing: right common iliac mass now 2.0 cm (increased from 1.6 cm), aortocaval conglomerate now 2.4 cm (increased from 2.1 cm), and a new 1.1 cm right obturator node identified

This pattern -- sustained and ongoing pulmonary response with concurrent and progressive nodal enlargement, now with a third nodal station involved -- is no longer consistent with pseudoprogression and represents true discordant response: partial response in the lungs, progressive disease in the retroperitoneum and pelvis.

At today's visit, the patient reports worsening fatigue over the past several weeks, which he grades as moderate. He has intermittent pelvic heaviness and mild low back discomfort he attributes to the pelvic nodal disease. He denies hematuria (consistent with post-nephrectomy status). He denies dyspnea or new respiratory symptoms. He continues on levothyroxine and denies symptoms of hypothyroidism. No rash, colitis, hepatitis, or pneumonitis symptoms. He denies visual changes, headache, or neurological symptoms.

Today's visit includes case presentation to the multidisciplinary genitourinary oncology tumor board (attended by medical oncology, interventional radiology, radiation oncology, and urology) to determine optimal next steps.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Moderate fatigue, weight stable (patient reports approximately 5 lb weight loss since last visit, now approximately 180 lbs). Appetite mildly reduced.
Head/Eyes/Ears/Nose/Throat: No headache, no visual changes, no epistaxis.
Respiratory: No dyspnea, no cough, no hemoptysis.
Cardiovascular: Mild exertional dyspnea with stairs (consistent with deconditioning and anemia). No chest pain, no palpitations, no lower extremity edema currently.
Gastrointestinal: Mild intermittent loose stools (1-2 per day above baseline, long-standing, consistent with prior grade 1 immune-mediated enteropathy). No frank colitis symptoms. No abdominal pain. No melena or hematochezia.
Genitourinary: No hematuria (nephrectomy site healing well). Mild pelvic heaviness/pressure. Voiding unremarkable.
Musculoskeletal: Low back discomfort, mild bilateral hip aching, consistent with bulky pelvic nodal disease. No focal bone pain.
Neurological: No weakness, no sensory changes, no bowel or bladder dysfunction.
Endocrine: No cold intolerance, no weight gain, no hair loss on current levothyroxine dose.
Dermatologic: No rash, no vitiligo (immunotherapy-related), no pruritus.
Psychiatric: Reports anxiety about treatment change, coping well with family support. No depression.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vital Signs: Blood pressure 134/82 mmHg, heart rate 78 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, SpO2 98% on room air, temperature 37.1°C. Weight: 180 lbs (down from 185 lbs at last visit).

General: Well-nourished, well-developed male appearing stated age. Alert and oriented x3. Mild fatigue apparent but in no acute distress. Ambulatory without assistance.

Head/Neck: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No lymphadenopathy in cervical, supraclavicular, or submandibular chains. Thyroid: no palpable enlargement, no nodules.

Chest/Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheeze, rales, or rhonchi. Normal respiratory effort.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No jugular venous distension. Peripheral pulses 2+ bilaterally.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. Right flank surgical scar well-healed, without signs of hernia or wound complication. No palpable hepatosplenomegaly. No palpable abdominal masses appreciated. Mild suprapubic fullness on deep palpation, consistent with pelvic nodal disease seen on imaging.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No signs of deep venous thrombosis. Skin intact without erythema or blistering on palms or soles (no hand-foot syndrome at present -- patient not on TKI).

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII grossly intact. Strength 5/5 in bilateral upper and lower extremities. Sensation intact to light touch. No ataxia.

Skin: No rash, no vitiligo, no hyperpigmentation. No immune-related cutaneous findings.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count:
- WBC: 7.1 K/uL (normal)
- Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild anemia, improved from 10.8 g/dL post-op)
- Platelets: 312 K/uL (normal; resolved thrombocytosis from initial diagnosis)
- ANC: 4.8 K/uL (normal)

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Sodium: 138 mEq/L, Potassium: 4.1 mEq/L
- Creatinine: 1.8 mg/dL (eGFR 42 mL/min/1.73m2, CKD stage 3b, stable post-nephrectomy)
- BUN: 24 mg/dL
- AST: 26 U/L, ALT: 29 U/L (both normal -- no immune hepatitis)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 88 U/L (normal)
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL (normal)
- Albumin: 3.6 g/dL (low normal; mild nutritional effect)
- Calcium: 9.4 mg/dL (normal; hypercalcemia resolved following nephrectomy and treatment)
- Glucose: 128 mg/dL (slightly elevated; consistent with type 2 diabetes on metformin)
- LDH: 241 U/L (mildly elevated above upper limit of normal, decreased from 287 at diagnosis)

Thyroid Function (most recent):
- TSH: 1.9 mIU/L (therapeutic, within normal range on levothyroxine 75 mcg)
- Free T4: 1.2 ng/dL (normal)

Coagulation: PT/INR within normal limits.

Urinalysis: No hematuria, no proteinuria (appropriate post-nephrectomy).

HbA1c: 7.4% (stable diabetic control).

---

IMAGING RESULTS

CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (interim restaging, approximately 6 months from immunotherapy initiation, reviewed in detail with radiology):

Lungs: The pulmonary target lesions continue to decrease in size and are clearly responding to immunotherapy. The right lower lobe nodule measures 0.7 cm (down from 1.4 cm at baseline, then 0.9 cm on first restaging). The right upper lobe nodule is 0.3 cm (down from 0.6 cm at baseline). The left lower lobe nodule is 0.7 cm (down from 0.8 cm). There is no new pulmonary nodule. No pleural effusion. No evidence of immune-related pneumonitis.

Retroperitoneum and Pelvis: The nodal disease is progressing despite the pulmonary response. The right common iliac nodal mass has grown from 1.6 cm on first restaging to 2.0 cm now. The aortocaval nodal conglomerate, which was 2.1 cm at first restaging, is now 2.4 cm. A new 1.1 cm right obturator node is identified that was not present on prior scans. The nodal involvement is now spreading further inferiorly into the pelvis, involving a third distinct nodal station.

Abdomen: No hepatic lesions. The nephrectomy bed appears unremarkable. No ascites.

Bone windows: No new convincing lytic or blastic lesions. No soft tissue masses in the bone.

Impression from radiology: Clear discordant response -- continued pulmonary improvement with active and progressing nodal disease including new pelvic nodal station involvement.

---

ASSESSMENT

Mr. Kowalski is a 63-year-old male with stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma, WHO/ISUP grade 4 with 22% sarcomatoid differentiation, IMDC intermediate risk, now approximately 6 months into first-line systemic therapy. His disease has manifested a persistently discordant response pattern that has become clearer over successive imaging: ongoing and deepening response in the pulmonary metastases (now >50% reduction from baseline in the dominant right lower lobe nodule), in the setting of concurrent and progressive nodal disease in the retroperitoneum and pelvis with expansion to a third nodal station (right obturator).

The biology of this discordant pattern merits discussion. The tumor carries co-loss of PBRM1 and BAP1, which is an uncommon and molecularly significant finding in ccRCC. BAP1-deficient tumors tend to be higher grade, more aggressive, and often have the sarcomatoid features seen here. PBRM1 and BAP1 mutations are thought to define distinct molecular subtypes of ccRCC with different immune microenvironments and possibly different sensitivities to immunotherapy. It is plausible that the conventional clear cell component (predominant in the pulmonary deposits) is responding to PD-1 blockade, while the sarcomatoid/BAP1-deficient subclone may be driving the nodal progression. This heterogeneity between metastatic sites has been observed in the literature and is a recognized challenge in managing sarcomatoid RCC.

From a clinical standpoint, the nodal progression is now clear and consecutive across two imaging timepoints over 6 weeks, including emergence of a new nodal station. This is no longer a diagnostic ambiguity regarding pseudoprogression -- the nodes are consistently, directionally growing.

The case was reviewed today with the multidisciplinary team. Radiation oncology was consulted regarding possible stereotactic body radiation therapy (SBRT) to the most prominent pelvic/retroperitoneal nodes in combination with continued systemic therapy, but given the multi-station, multi-node involvement (now 4 distinct nodes/nodal masses in 3 anatomic regions), this approach was felt to be not feasible for comprehensive control. Continuing Opdivo alone without modification -- given the established and ongoing nodal progression at this point -- is no longer supportable as the sole strategy.

MDT consensus recommendation: Continue nivo for an additional 6 weeks with a repeat CT at that time. If the nodal disease continues to grow, transition to second-line cabozantinib. Rationale for this approach: (1) the pulmonary response on Opdivo is meaningful and durable, and abrupt discontinuation may lead to pulmonary rebound; (2) cabozantinib's activity is well-established in second-line ccRCC (METEOR trial data) and has particular mechanistic rationale given the patient's MET amplification on NGS (cabozantinib targets VEGFR2, MET, and AXL); (3) another short observation window is acceptable given the patient's maintained ECOG 1 status, absence of symptoms directly threatening end-organ function, and the fact that nodal progression, while clear, is not yet causing significant lymphedema or ureteral obstruction.

The patient was counseled in detail about this recommendation, including the high likelihood that cabozantinib will be needed at the next assessment based on current trajectory. He understands and agrees with the plan. He expressed concern about the discordant nature of his response and was reassured that this pattern, while unusual, is recognized in sarcomatoid RCC treated with immunotherapy, and that cabozantinib has demonstrated responses in this setting.

PROBLEM LIST:
1. Metastatic clear cell RCC, grade 4, 22% sarcomatoid differentiation -- discordant response on Opdivo maintenance; pulmonary disease responding, nodal disease progressing
2. Immune-related hypothyroidism, grade 2 -- managed, TSH therapeutic on levothyroxine 75 mcg daily
3. Hypertension -- managed with lisinopril; BP well controlled
4. Type 2 diabetes mellitus -- managed with metformin; HbA1c stable
5. CKD stage 3b (post-nephrectomy) -- creatinine stable at 1.8 mg/dL
6. Moderate fatigue -- multifactorial (anemia, disease burden, ongoing immunotherapy)
7. Mild anemia -- Hgb 11.4, likely chronic disease component; monitoring

---

PLAN

1. SYSTEMIC THERAPY: Continue Opdivo 480 mg IV flat dose every 4 weeks. Next infusion as scheduled. This will be the last observation window before likely transition to cabozantinib if nodal progression confirmed.

2. RESTAGING IMAGING: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast in approximately 6 weeks. If nodal disease continues to progress, cabozantinib will be initiated. Patient has been counseled that this is the expected trajectory and we want to proceed when clinically indicated.

3. ANTICIPATED TRANSITION TO CABOZANTINIB: Discussed in detail today. Standard starting dose 60 mg orally once daily for ccRCC. Key anticipated toxicities reviewed with patient: hand-foot syndrome, diarrhea, hypertension (particularly relevant given his history), fatigue, and mucositis. Given existing hypertension, antihypertensive regimen will be proactively reviewed and likely augmented at initiation. Referral to pharmacy for TKI counseling and GI nursing for side effect management will be placed at transition.

4. HYPOTHYROIDISM: Continue levothyroxine 75 mcg daily. TSH therapeutic. Recheck thyroid function in 3 months or sooner if symptoms develop.

5. ANEMIA: Hemoglobin 11.4 g/dL, chronic and stable. No intervention at this time. Monitor. Iron studies to be checked at next visit to rule out iron deficiency component.

6. DIABETES / HYPERTENSION: Continue metformin and lisinopril at current doses. BP well controlled today. Note: if transitioning to cabozantinib, lisinopril dose may require uptitration given TKI-related hypertension; will reassess at that time.

7. RENAL FUNCTION: Creatinine stable at 1.8 (CKD 3b). Nephrology co-management continues. Metformin dose acceptable at current eGFR; monitor.

8. PERFORMANCE STATUS: KPS 75, ECOG 1. Patient maintaining ambulatory function and partial activities. Encouraged to continue light physical activity as tolerated. Referral to physical therapy / cancer rehabilitation for fatigue management discussed; patient deferred at this time.

9. PSYCHOSOCIAL: Patient and spouse engaged; appropriate anxiety regarding uncertain trajectory. Continued discussion about prognosis and treatment options. Social work referral available if needed. Patient enrolled in survivorship program.

10. RETURN TO CLINIC: In 6 weeks for post-imaging assessment and treatment planning.

Time spent with patient: approximately 50 minutes, greater than 50% of which was counseling and coordination of care.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Medical Oncologist, GU Oncology Program]
